provenance.source_corpus: Ten year responses of Engelmann spruce and a high-elevation Ericaceous shrub community to manual cutting treatments in southern interior British Columbia

source_spans.text: Manual cutting increased Shannon diversity in the first year post-treatment (p = 0.039), after which it returned to control levels.
Spruce survival was significantly higher in the treatment than the control 3 years after brushing (p = 0.020).
The cutting treatment significantly increased ground-level diameter after 3 and 5 years, but not after 10 years.
After 10 years, shrub cover was still lower in the cutting treatment than the control, but height had recovered within 3 years.
Manual cutting increased Shannon diversity in the first year post-treatment (p = 0.039), after which it returned to control levels.
There were no differences in species richness.

Both non-linear (Table 4 ) and linear (data not shown) models revealed a negative relationship between first year total shrub or summed Ericaceous shrub abundance (COV TS1,C O V ES1, CRH TS1, CRHES1) and spruce diameter
vegetation competition was still affecting at least some surviving seedlings when they were 10–12 years old.
